registry.description: Contrition is the genuine, deep sorrow of someone who has done wrong and knows it — not the surface shame of being caught but the interior grief of having actually caused harm or broken trust. The biblical vocabulary gives this its strongest expression in the psalms of repentance: the broken and contrite heart, the crushed spirit. Contrition is the inner precondition for genuine repentance: without it, repentance is merely a change of strategy rather than a change of heart. [Covered by repentance (#135), brokenness (#18).]

groups.context_description: inner-being anguish — the crushing of the person by suffering, adversity, or hostile words
substitutionary crushing — the Servant's inner person crushed for iniquity, producing peace and healing
the contrite and crushed inner spirit — the condition of brokenness before God that occasions divine presence, and its refusal
the crushed and contrite inner spirit — the broken posture before God that draws divine nearness and revival
the crushed/contrite inner spirit — brokenness before God drawing divine nearness
groups.notes: Homograph sub-entry of H1793A dak.ka (contrite). Same verses — Psa 34:18 and Isa 57:15. See SBF-036-001 for Session B assessment of whether this sub-entry carries a distinct inner-being nuance.

groups.context_description: the broken and contrite heart as the inner sacrifice acceptable to God — inner brokenness in penitence and suffering
corporate inner desolation under divine crushing — the community broken by God in distress
inner terror and panic — the inner state of dismay accompanying devastating defeat
the humble and contrite spirit — the broken inner posture before God that receives divine attention

senses.sense_text: to crush, be crushed, be contrite, be broken
(Hithpael) to allow oneself to be crushed
to crush, be crushed, be contrite, be broken
(Niphal) to be crushed, be contrite, be broken
beaten out, pure, pounded fine (in a mortar), costly
to beat, crush by beating, crush to pieces, crush fine

7552.verse_text: Job 4:19 how much more those who dwell in houses of clay , whose foundation is in the dust , who are crushed like the moth .
7552.notes: Crushed like the moth — mortal frailty metaphor

7552.verse_text: Job 5:4 His children are far from safety ; they are crushed in the gate , and there is no one to deliver them.
7552.notes: Children crushed in the gate — external fate/oppression

7552.verse_text: Job 6:9 that it would please God to crush me, that he would let loose his hand and cut me off !
7552.notes: Job's longing for God to crush and end his suffering — existential anguish

7552.verse_text: Job 19:2 “ How long will you torment me and break me in pieces with words ?
7552.notes: Broken in pieces by words — inner torment from verbal assault

7552.verse_text: Job 22:9 You have sent widows away empty , and the arms of the fatherless were crushed .
7552.notes: Arms of the fatherless crushed — external oppression

7552.verse_text: Job 34:25 Thus , knowing their works , he overturns them in the night , and they are crushed .
7552.notes: Divine judgment overturning the wicked — external judicial act

7552.verse_text: Psa 72:4 May he defend the cause of the poor of the people , give deliverance to the children of the needy , and crush the oppressor !
7552.notes: Messianic king crushing the oppressor — external judicial act

7552.verse_text: Psa 89:10 You crushed Rahab like a carcass ; you scattered your enemies with your mighty arm .
Psa 94:5 They crush your people , O Lord , and afflict your heritage .
Psa 143:3 For the enemy has pursued my soul ; he has crushed my life to the ground ; he has made me sit in darkness like those long dead .
Pro 22:22 Do not rob the poor , because he is poor , or crush the afflicted at the gate ,
7552.notes: Prohibition against crushing the afflicted — external act

7552.verse_text: Isa 3:15 What do you mean by crushing my people , by grinding the face of the poor ?” declares the Lord God of hosts .
Isa 19:10 Those who are the pillars of the land will be crushed , and all who work for pay will be grieved .
7552.notes: Pillars of the land crushed — societal/structural crushing

7552.verse_text: Isa 53:5 But he was pierced for our transgressions ; he was crushed for our iniquities ; upon him was the chastisement that brought us peace , and with his wounds we are healed .

Isa 53:10 Yet it was the will of the Lord to crush him; he has put him to grief ; when his soul makes an offering for guilt , he shall see his offspring ; he shall prolong his days ; the will of the Lord shall prosper in his hand .
7552.notes: Will of Lord to crush him; soul as guilt offering

7552.verse_text: Isa 57:15 For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity , whose name is Holy : “I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with him who is of a contrite and lowly spirit , to revive the spirit of the lowly , and to revive the heart of the contrite .
Jer 44:10 They have not humbled themselves even to this day , nor have they feared , nor walked in my law and my statutes that I set before you and before your fathers .
7552.notes: Failure to humble — the unbroken inner posture refusing contrition

7552.verse_text: Lam 3:34 To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the earth ,
7552.notes: To crush underfoot all the prisoners — external crushing

7553.verse_text: Psa 34:18 The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit .

Isa 57:15 For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity , whose name is Holy : “I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with him who is of a contrite and lowly spirit , to revive the spirit of the lowly , and to revive the heart of the contrite .
7553.notes: God dwells with the contrite and lowly spirit

7557.verse_text: Psa 34:18 The Lord is near to the brokenhearted and saves the crushed in spirit .

Isa 57:15 For thus says the One who is high and lifted up, who inhabits eternity , whose name is Holy : “I dwell in the high and holy place, and also with him who is of a contrite and lowly spirit , to revive the spirit of the lowly , and to revive the heart of the contrite .

7554.verse_text: Psa 10:10 The helpless are crushed , sink down, and fall by his might .
7554.notes: Helpless crushed by the wicked — external oppression

7554.verse_text: Psa 38:8 I am feeble and crushed ; I groan because of the tumult of my heart .
7554.notes: Feeble and crushed; groaning because of the tumult of the heart

7554.verse_text: Psa 44:19 yet you have broken us in the place of jackals and covered us with the shadow of death .
Psa 51:8 Let me hear joy and gladness ; let the bones that you have broken rejoice .
7554.notes: Bones broken by God; seeking joy and gladness after inner brokenness

7554.verse_text: Psa 51:17 The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit ; a broken and contrite heart , O God , you will not despise .

7558.verse_text: Deu 23:1 “No one whose testicles are crushed or whose male organ is cut off shall enter the assembly of the Lord .

7563.verse_text: Exo 27:20 “ You shall command the people of Israel that they bring to you pure beaten olive oil for the light , that a lamp may regularly be set up to burn .
7563.notes: Beaten olive oil for the lamp — cultic/agricultural

7563.verse_text: Exo 29:40 And with the first lamb a tenth measure of fine flour mingled with a fourth of a hin of beaten oil , and a fourth of a hin of wine for a drink offering .
7563.notes: Beaten oil in grain offering — cultic/agricultural

7563.verse_text: Lev 24:2 “ Command the people of Israel to bring you pure oil from beaten olives for the lamp , that a light may be kept burning regularly .
7563.notes: Pure oil from beaten olives for lamp — cultic

7563.verse_text: Num 28:5 also a tenth of an ephah of fine flour for a grain offering , mixed with a quarter of a hin of beaten oil .
7563.notes: Beaten oil in grain offering — cultic

7563.verse_text: 1Ki 5:11 while Solomon gave Hiram 20,000 cors of wheat as food for his household , and 20,000 cors of beaten oil . Solomon gave this to Hiram year by year .

7556.verse_text: Lev 22:24 Any animal that has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut you shall not offer to the Lord ; you shall not do it within your land ,
Num 14:45 Then the Amalekites and the Canaanites who lived in that hill country came down and defeated them and pursued them, even to Hormah .
Deu 1:44 Then the Amorites who lived in that hill country came out against you and chased you as bees do and beat you down in Seir as far as Hormah .

Deu 9:21 Then I took the sinful thing, the calf that you had made , and burned it with fire and crushed it, grinding it very small , until it was as fine as dust . And I threw the dust of it into the brook that ran down from the mountain .
7556.notes: Moses crushing the golden calf to dust — physical destruction

7556.verse_text: 2Ki 18:4 He removed the high places and broke the pillars and cut down the Asherah . And he broke in pieces the bronze serpent that Moses had made , for until those days the people of Israel had made offerings to it (it was called Nehushtan ).
7556.notes: Hezekiah breaking the bronze serpent — physical destruction of idol

7556.verse_text: 2Ch 15:6 They were broken in pieces. Nation was crushed by nation and city by city , for God troubled them with every sort of distress .
7556.notes: Nation crushed by nation — societal/military devastation

7556.verse_text: 2Ch 34:7 he broke down the altars and beat the Asherim and the images into powder and cut down all the incense altars throughout all the land of Israel . Then he returned to Jerusalem .
7556.notes: Josiah crushing idols to powder — physical destruction

7556.verse_text: Job 4:20 Between morning and evening they are beaten to pieces ; they perish forever without anyone regarding it.
7556.notes: Beaten to pieces — human frailty/mortality image

7556.verse_text: Psa 89:23 I will crush his foes before him and strike down those who hate him.
7556.notes: God crushing enemies — external divine act

7556.verse_text: Isa 2:4 He shall judge between the nations , and shall decide disputes for many peoples ; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares , and their spears into pruning hooks ; nation shall not lift up sword against nation , neither shall they learn war anymore .
7556.notes: Swords into plowshares — ka.tat carries mechanical transformation; inner peace orientation carried by passage context, not term

7556.verse_text: Isa 24:12 Desolation is left in the city ; the gates are battered into ruins .
7556.notes: Gates battered into ruins — structural destruction

7556.verse_text: Isa 30:14 and its breaking is like that of a potter’s vessel that is smashed so ruthlessly that among its fragments not a shard is found with which to take fire from the hearth , or to dip up water out of the cistern .”
7556.notes: Potter's vessel smashed — physical destruction metaphor

7556.verse_text: Jer 46:5 Why have I seen it? They are dismayed and have turned backward . Their warriors are beaten down and have fled in haste ; they look not back — terror on every side ! declares the Lord .
Joe 3:10 Beat your plowshares into swords , and your pruning hooks into spears ; let the weak say , “ I am a warrior .”
7556.notes: Beat plowshares into swords — inverse mobilisation; same logic as Isa 2:4

7556.verse_text: Mic 1:7 All her carved images shall be beaten to pieces, all her wages shall be burned with fire , and all her idols I will lay waste , for from the fee of a prostitute she gathered them, and to the fee of a prostitute they shall return .
7556.notes: Carved images beaten to pieces — physical idol destruction

7556.verse_text: Mic 4:3 He shall judge between many peoples , and shall decide disputes for strong nations far away; and they shall beat their swords into plowshares , and their spears into pruning hooks ; nation shall not lift up sword against nation , neither shall they learn war anymore ;
7556.notes: Swords into plowshares (Mic 4:3) — same logic as Isa 2:4

7556.verse_text: Zec 11:6 For I will no longer have pity on the inhabitants of this land , declares the Lord . Behold , I will cause each of them to fall into the hand of his neighbor , and each into the hand of his king , and they shall crush the land , and I will deliver none from their hand .”
7556.notes: Crushing the land through judgment — external

7564.verse_text: Isa 30:14 and its breaking is like that of a potter’s vessel that is smashed so ruthlessly that among its fragments not a shard is found with which to take fire from the hearth , or to dip up water out of the cistern .”
7564.notes: Physical fragments of broken vessel — no inner-being content

7562.verse_text: Psa 35:15 But at my stumbling they rejoiced and gathered ; they gathered together against me; wretches whom I did not know tore at me without ceasing ;
7562.notes: Enemies gathering against the psalmist — ne.kheh inner-being relevance not evident

7555.verse_text: 2Sa 4:4 Jonathan , the son of Saul , had a son who was crippled in his feet . He was five years old when the news about Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel , and his nurse took him up and fled , and as she fled in her haste , he fell and became lame . And his name was Mephibosheth .
7555.notes: Mephibosheth crippled in his feet — physical disability

7555.verse_text: 2Sa 9:3 And the king said , “Is there not still someone of the house of Saul , that I may show the kindness of God to him?” Ziba said to the king , “There is still a son of Jonathan ; he is crippled in his feet .”
7555.notes: Mephibosheth crippled in his feet — physical disability

7555.verse_text: Isa 66:2 All these things my hand has made , and so all these things came to be, declares the Lord . But this is the one to whom I will look : he who is humble and contrite in spirit and trembles at my word .

open_flags.description: Registry 30 (contrition) produces strong convergence: 5 of 9 groups assign Dependence / Creatureliness (7552-003, 7553-001, 7554-001, 7555-001, 7557-001). This convergence suggests contrition is the penitential sub-form of Dependence / Creatureliness — brokenness before God arising from moral self-awareness and recognition of unworthiness. Session D should examine whether the programme vocabulary needs a finer distinction between general dependence/trust and specifically penitential dependence (contrition). Connect with Reg 10 (dependence/trust) and Reg 80 (trust) in the programme.

generic.section: Section 1 — Generic (gap addition R067 obslog v3)
generic.question_text: For registries where verse context groups carry affective inner-being states (gladness, well-being, shalom-condition) that do not fit Moral Character, Cognition, or Volition, what dimension should be assigned? The current 10-dimension vocabulary may require an Experiential/Affective category for these groups.

generic.section: Section 1 — Generic (gap addition R067 obslog v3)
generic.question_text: For verse context groups that name inner affective well-being states (glad of heart, shalom-condition, prospering inwardly) that are not primarily moral assessments, cognitive evaluations, or volitional acts, should the dimension be Emotion — Positive or Vitality / Existence? The two dimensions need clearer boundary criteria for these borderline cases.

generic.section: Section 1 — Generic (gap addition R067 obslog v3)
generic.question_text: For verse context groups where God's inner-being engagement is a creative-constitutive evaluative act (declaring creation good, establishing ontological goodness) rather than a relational disposition toward humanity, should Dimension 11 (Divine-Human Correspondence) apply, or should the dimension review introduce a distinct label for this ontological-creative register?
Where the word has multiple distinct semantic modes, does the verse evidence reveal a unified inner logic that holds the modes together — or are they genuinely independent phenomena?
Does the word carry a structural negative or absence form — and if so, does the negative form engage the same inner-being faculty as the positive?
What is the structural disposition of the word — where does it originate?
What determines whether the word is extended or withheld?
What are the distinct modes of operation of the word in the inner being?
What does the word produce in the inner being of the recipient?
What does the word produce in the relational position of the recipient toward another?
What does the word reveal about the disposition of God toward the human being?
What does the word reveal about the basis on which God's disposition toward a person is established?
Where, somatically or relationally, does the word locate the reality of its operation in the giver and in the recipient?
Does the word describe a one-time act or an ongoing condition — and how are these distinguished?
What spatial or directional language is used to describe the ongoing condition the word produces?
Does the word produce enabling capacity in the inner being — and if so, what kind?
What character quality does the word produce in the inner being?
How does the word express itself in relation to others?
What inner condition or orientation is identified as the ground of genuine expression of the word?
Is there a discernible sequence or movement in the way the word operates through the inner person?
What direction of movement does the word follow within and through the inner person?
What does the word identify as its originating source?
What does the word reveal about the origin of the human capacity to seek it?
Does the word share an etymological root with another inner-being characteristic — and if so, what is that characteristic?
What does the word produce as a natural inner response in the one who receives it?
Where the word has both a positive (presence) and negative (absence/not-word) register, what does the negative register reveal about the inner-being mechanisms of the positive?
Does the verse evidence distinguish between the inner quality of goodness and the external assessment of conduct as good — and what is the relationship between them?
What inner-being logic or orientation functions as the structural opposite of the word?
What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to receive the word?
What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to extend the word to others?
What is the inner-being condition of the person who has received the word but failed to recognise it?
What inner-being experience cultivates the capacity to extend the word to others?
What inner-being posture does the act of earnest appeal express?
What somatic forms does the inner act of seeking the word take?
What inner-being orientation closes off the reception of the word?
What relational orientation toward others diminishes the operation of the word?
What is the relationship between the word and conditions of weakness in the inner being?
What kind of inner-being transformation does the word produce — does it change the condition or the person's orientation to the condition?
What is the first inner-being response to receiving the word unexpectedly?
What is the sequence of inner-being states as the word takes hold in the person?
In the eschatological context, what is the first inner-being response to the full outpouring of the word?
What is the relationship between mourning and the full reception of the word in the inner being?
What quality of inner-being stability does the word produce?
What orientation does the stability produced by the word carry?
What capacity toward others does the word produce in the inner being of the one who has received it?
What is the inner-being source of the capacity to extend the word to others — is it discipline or transformation?
How does the word relate to genuine human effort — does it displace it, supplement it, or reconstitute it?
What does the word reveal about the inner-being condition of the person who does not receive it?
What does the word's primary verse reveal about its essential character?
What does the primary verse identify as the relationship between the human version and the divine version of the word?
What does the primary verse identify as the primary subject of the word — who exercises it?
Does the primary verse name more than one function or dimension of the word — and if so, what is the relationship between them?
What does the primary verse reveal about the breadth of those toward whom the word operates?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the direction of the word's operation?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to suffering or affliction?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to moral failure or guilt?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to covenantal relationship?
What reason does a key verse give for the extension of the word — in the recipient's actions, or in the character of the one who gives it?
Where does a key verse locate the reality of the word — in a specific faculty, organ, or location of the giver?
What practical outcome does a key verse show the word producing for the recipient?
What inner state does a key verse identify as the first response to receiving the word?
What comes first in a key verse — the establishment of the relationship or standing the word confers, or the commission or responsibility that follows from it?
What does a key verse identify as the concrete content of what the word gives?
What spatial or directional language does a key verse use for the reality of the word's operation?
Does any verse distinguish between the means of entry into the word and the condition the word produces?
Does any verse describe the present condition produced by the word as carrying a forward orientation?
What does a key verse identify as the ground of the speaker's or recipient's identity — achievement, reception, or something else?
How does a key verse handle the co-presence of genuine human effort and prior divine initiative?
In what form does a key verse present the word — and what does that form enable?
What effect does the word in communicative form produce in those who receive it?
Does a key verse describe the word's work as directed toward the present only, or toward a future state?
Does any verse distinguish between a performed version and a genuine version of the word?
What inner orientation does a key verse identify as the source of the genuine quality?
What is the sequence of somatic and verbal response in a key verse — which comes first?
What does a recipient's response in a key verse reveal about their inner experience of receiving the word?
What structural condition in a key verse establishes the recipient as having no basis for the word they receive?
In a key verse, does the word operate within or across existing relational, covenantal, or social boundaries?
What is the logical structure of a key argument about the word — and what is its premise and conclusion?
What inner-being state does the argument structure in a key verse produce in the recipient?
What is the relationship between the divine act and the human act in a key verse — illustrative or causal?
Where does a key verse locate the inner quality from which the word's outward act flows?
What does a verse about the withdrawal or absence of the word reveal about the word's role?
What does a verse about the word in extremity reveal about its depth of operation?
What does a verse about the word in the judicial or accountability context reveal about its function?
What does a verse about the word in the supplication context reveal about what the inner person knows about itself?
What does a verse about the word in the worship or liturgical context reveal about the word's function in ordered relationship with God?
What does a verse about the word at the level of covenantal promise reveal about its scope and duration?
What does a verse about the word in the context of moral collapse reveal about the word's place in the structure of the inner life?
What does a verse about the word in the context of formation or sanctification reveal about how the word shapes the person over time?
What does a verse about the word in the context of community or fellowship reveal about the word's social dimension?
What does a verse about the word in the context of eschatology or final things reveal about the word's ultimate trajectory?
What does a verse about the word's physical or somatic expression reveal about the inner-outer relationship in the word's operation?
Where a registry contains multiple anchor verses across multiple groups, is there a primary or controlling anchor — and how is it identified?

Does the comparative or evaluative mode of the word (where the word functions as a ranking operator rather than naming a characteristic) constitute a distinct analytical case requiring separate treatment?

Where the programme has distributed a root family across multiple registries, what does that distribution reveal about the programme's analytical framework, and does the distribution reflect genuine semantic distinctions or is it a programme boundary decision requiring Session D review?
What is the total number of terms in this registry, and how are they distributed between Hebrew and Greek?
What is the sharing ratio — what proportion of terms are shared with adjacent word studies?
What is the primary Hebrew term, and what does its root meaning reveal about the word's essential character?
What is the primary Greek term, and what does its range of use reveal?
Does the vocabulary distinguish between the word as an inner disposition and the word as an outward act — and how?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the word as a settled character quality?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the one who habitually exercises the word — a person-type term?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the word's absence or structural opposite?
What does the relationship between the root meaning and the primary sense reveal about the word's conceptual origin?
Does the vocabulary include terms that name the word as something received passively and terms that name it as something actively exercised?
Does any term in the vocabulary carry both the sense of the word in its human expression and its divine expression?
What does the LXX use of the vocabulary reveal about the continuity or development of the word across the Testaments?
Is there a named failure-mode term in the vocabulary — a term for the person who has received the word but withholds it from others?
Does the vocabulary include a term newly coined in the NT period for this word — and if so, what does the coinage reveal?
What does the verbal form of the primary term reveal about the word's mode of operation?
Does the vocabulary include a spatial or architectural term — a place or structure associated with the word?
What does the semantic range of the primary term reveal about the breadth of the word's operation?
Does any term carry a somatic or physiological dimension — naming an inner organ or physical site as the word's location?
What does the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective) reveal about how the word is conceptualised — as object, action, or quality?
Does the vocabulary include a supplication term — a term for the act of seeking the word from another?
What does the supplication term reveal about the inner posture required to seek the word?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the mechanism by which the word is administered or conveyed?
What does the atonement or propitiation vocabulary (if present) reveal about the structural conditions under which the word operates?
Does any term reveal a classical-to-NT directional reversal — a term whose direction of operation changes between its classical use and its NT use?
What does the vocabulary reveal about whether the word operates primarily within or across relational boundaries?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's relationship to covenant — is it bounded by covenant or can it extend beyond?
Does any term in the vocabulary name the word as a divine possession — something that belongs to God and is located with him?
What does the root architecture of the vocabulary reveal about the word's conceptual relationship to adjacent characteristics?
Does the vocabulary include compound terms — and if so, what do the compounds reveal?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's temporal character — is it punctiliar, durative, or both?
Does the vocabulary reveal whether the word operates as a judicial category, a relational one, or both?
What does the most intensive or plural form of the vocabulary term reveal?
Does the vocabulary include terms that name the word's expression in speech?
Does the vocabulary include terms whose etymology connects to the body — and if so, to which part?
What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about the word's complete semantic range — from its most intimate to its most structural expression?
Does the vocabulary include a term that names the word's operation at the intersection of the human and divine — a term that holds both simultaneously?
What is the strongest co-occurrence connection for this word — which adjacent characteristic appears most frequently in the same verses — and what does the co-occurrence pattern reveal?
Does any verse indicate that this word and its most closely connected adjacent characteristic operate simultaneously in the same inner-being moment — and what would that simultaneity imply?
What are the three highest vocabulary-sharing registries for this word — and what does the pattern of sharing reveal about the word's structural position in the programme?
Does the vocabulary overlap with any adjacent characteristic extend to root-level architecture — a shared root rather than only shared individual terms?
How many of this word's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another cluster or characteristic — and is there any cluster that shares all confirmed dimensions?
Does any single verse make an explicit structural relationship between this word and an adjacent characteristic — naming the connection rather than merely exemplifying it?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and the characteristic with which it shares the most anchor verses — and what does that shared anchoring reveal?

Where this word and an adjacent characteristic share a dominant directionality (such as both being primarily divine-to-human), what does the shared directionality reveal about their structural relationship?
What is the relationship between this word and human will in the verse evidence — does the word displace, bypass, or reconstitute the will?
How does the study describe the relationship between this word and calling — is calling a consequence, an expression, or a distinct phenomenon?
What inner-being posture connects this word and listening — and is that posture described as a precondition or an expression of the word?
How does the study distinguish this word from faith — and what is their structural relationship?
What is the described sequence from the word to peace — and what comes between them?
What is the vocabulary overlap and co-occurrence count between this word and guilt?
What is the relationship between this word and mourning — does mourning precede, follow, or accompany the word?
Does the study present this word and hope as two separate phenomena or as aspects of a single inner-being posture?
Does any verse function as a primary anchor in both this word's study and another word's study — and what does that dual membership reveal about their relationship?
Does any verse anchor both this word and another word — and what shared inner-being posture does that dual anchoring reveal?
Does any verse present adjacent qualities as aspects of a unified inner-being posture — and if so, what is their common source?
What functional overlap does the verse evidence suggest between this word and wisdom?
Does the word operate only in relation to moral conditions (guilt) or also in relation to conditions of incapacity — and what is the evidence?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and goodness — and in what literary context does the pairing appear?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and anointing, and between this word and blessing?
Is the connection between this word and forgiveness established through contextual inference or through the lexical form of the term itself?
Does the verse evidence in this study point toward a connection with repentance — and is that connection sufficiently examined or deferred?
Does a recurring posture appear across the word-seeking scenes in the study — and is its relationship to an adjacent characteristic examined or deferred?

Where a registry's word functions in more than one dimension (both as a character attribute and as a comparative/evaluative operator), does the co-occurrence and correlation data reveal different connection patterns for different modes of the word?

The two dimensions need clearer boundary criteria for these borderline cases.

Section 1 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where the word has multiple distinct semantic modes, does the verse evidence reveal a unified inner logic that holds the modes together — or are they genuinely independent phenomena?
Does the word carry a structural negative or absence form — and if so, does the negative form engage the same inner-being faculty as the positive?

Section 1 — Word Characteristic Summary.question_text: What is the structural disposition of the word — where does it originate?
What determines whether the word is extended or withheld?
What are the distinct modes of operation of the word in the inner being?
What does the word produce in the inner being of the recipient?
What does the word produce in the relational position of the recipient toward another?
What does the word reveal about the disposition of God toward the human being?
What does the word reveal about the basis on which God's disposition toward a person is established?
Where, somatically or relationally, does the word locate the reality of its operation in the giver and in the recipient?
Does the word describe a one-time act or an ongoing condition — and how are these distinguished?
What spatial or directional language is used to describe the ongoing condition the word produces?
Does the word produce enabling capacity in the inner being — and if so, what kind?
What character quality does the word produce in the inner being?
How does the word express itself in relation to others?
What inner condition or orientation is identified as the ground of genuine expression of the word?
Is there a discernible sequence or movement in the way the word operates through the inner person?
What direction of movement does the word follow within and through the inner person?
What does the word identify as its originating source?
What does the word reveal about the origin of the human capacity to seek it?
Does the word share an etymological root with another inner-being characteristic — and if so, what is that characteristic?
What does the word produce as a natural inner response in the one who receives it?

Section 2 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where the word has both a positive (presence) and negative (absence/not-word) register, what does the negative register reveal about the inner-being mechanisms of the positive?
Does the verse evidence distinguish between the inner quality of goodness and the external assessment of conduct as good — and what is the relationship between them?

Section 2 — Word Impact Description.question_text: What inner-being logic or orientation functions as the structural opposite of the word?
What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to receive the word?
What effect does the logic of merit have on the inner being's capacity to extend the word to others?
What is the inner-being condition of the person who has received the word but failed to recognise it?
What inner-being experience cultivates the capacity to extend the word to others?
What inner-being posture does the act of earnest appeal express?
What somatic forms does the inner act of seeking the word take?
What inner-being orientation closes off the reception of the word?
What relational orientation toward others diminishes the operation of the word?
What is the relationship between the word and conditions of weakness in the inner being?
What kind of inner-being transformation does the word produce — does it change the condition or the person's orientation to the condition?
What is the first inner-being response to receiving the word unexpectedly?
What is the sequence of inner-being states as the word takes hold in the person?
In the eschatological context, what is the first inner-being response to the full outpouring of the word?
What is the relationship between mourning and the full reception of the word in the inner being?
What quality of inner-being stability does the word produce?
What orientation does the stability produced by the word carry?
What capacity toward others does the word produce in the inner being of the one who has received it?
What is the inner-being source of the capacity to extend the word to others — is it discipline or transformation?
How does the word relate to genuine human effort — does it displace it, supplement it, or reconstitute it?
What does the word reveal about the inner-being condition of the person who does not receive it?

Section 3 — Annotated Verse Evidence.question_text: What does the word's primary verse reveal about its essential character?
What does the primary verse identify as the relationship between the human version and the divine version of the word?
What does the primary verse identify as the primary subject of the word — who exercises it?
Does the primary verse name more than one function or dimension of the word — and if so, what is the relationship between them?
What does the primary verse reveal about the breadth of those toward whom the word operates?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the direction of the word's operation?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to suffering or affliction?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to moral failure or guilt?
What does the verse evidence reveal about the word's relationship to covenantal relationship?
What reason does a key verse give for the extension of the word — in the recipient's actions, or in the character of the one who gives it?
Where does a key verse locate the reality of the word — in a specific faculty, organ, or location of the giver?
What practical outcome does a key verse show the word producing for the recipient?
What inner state does a key verse identify as the first response to receiving the word?
What comes first in a key verse — the establishment of the relationship or standing the word confers, or the commission or responsibility that follows from it?
What does a key verse identify as the concrete content of what the word gives?
What spatial or directional language does a key verse use for the reality of the word's operation?
Does any verse distinguish between the means of entry into the word and the condition the word produces?
Does any verse describe the present condition produced by the word as carrying a forward orientation?
What does a key verse identify as the ground of the speaker's or recipient's identity — achievement, reception, or something else?
How does a key verse handle the co-presence of genuine human effort and prior divine initiative?
In what form does a key verse present the word — and what does that form enable?
What effect does the word in communicative form produce in those who receive it?
Does a key verse describe the word's work as directed toward the present only, or toward a future state?
Does any verse distinguish between a performed version and a genuine version of the word?
What inner orientation does a key verse identify as the source of the genuine quality?
What is the sequence of somatic and verbal response in a key verse — which comes first?
What does a recipient's response in a key verse reveal about their inner experience of receiving the word?
What structural condition in a key verse establishes the recipient as having no basis for the word they receive?
In a key verse, does the word operate within or across existing relational, covenantal, or social boundaries?
What is the logical structure of a key argument about the word — and what is its premise and conclusion?
What inner-being state does the argument structure in a key verse produce in the recipient?
What is the relationship between the divine act and the human act in a key verse — illustrative or causal?
Where does a key verse locate the inner quality from which the word's outward act flows?
What does a verse about the withdrawal or absence of the word reveal about the word's role?
What does a verse about the word in extremity reveal about its depth of operation?
What does a verse about the word in the judicial or accountability context reveal about its function?
What does a verse about the word in the supplication context reveal about what the inner person knows about itself?
What does a verse about the word in the worship or liturgical context reveal about the word's function in ordered relationship with God?
What does a verse about the word at the level of covenantal promise reveal about its scope and duration?
What does a verse about the word in the context of moral collapse reveal about the word's place in the structure of the inner life?
What does a verse about the word in the context of formation or sanctification reveal about how the word shapes the person over time?
What does a verse about the word in the context of community or fellowship reveal about the word's social dimension?
What does a verse about the word in the context of eschatology or final things reveal about the word's ultimate trajectory?
What does a verse about the word's physical or somatic expression reveal about the inner-outer relationship in the word's operation?

Section 3 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where a registry contains multiple anchor verses across multiple groups, is there a primary or controlling anchor — and how is it identified?

Does the comparative or evaluative mode of the word (where the word functions as a ranking operator rather than naming a characteristic) constitute a distinct analytical case requiring separate treatment?

Section 4 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where the programme has distributed a root family across multiple registries, what does that distribution reveal about the programme's analytical framework, and does the distribution reflect genuine semantic distinctions or is it a programme boundary decision requiring Session D review?

Section 4 — Original Language Vocabulary.question_text: What is the total number of terms in this registry, and how are they distributed between Hebrew and Greek?
What is the sharing ratio — what proportion of terms are shared with adjacent word studies?
What is the primary Hebrew term, and what does its root meaning reveal about the word's essential character?
What is the primary Greek term, and what does its range of use reveal?
Does the vocabulary distinguish between the word as an inner disposition and the word as an outward act — and how?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the word as a settled character quality?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the one who habitually exercises the word — a person-type term?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the word's absence or structural opposite?
What does the relationship between the root meaning and the primary sense reveal about the word's conceptual origin?
Does the vocabulary include terms that name the word as something received passively and terms that name it as something actively exercised?
Does any term in the vocabulary carry both the sense of the word in its human expression and its divine expression?
What does the LXX use of the vocabulary reveal about the continuity or development of the word across the Testaments?
Is there a named failure-mode term in the vocabulary — a term for the person who has received the word but withholds it from others?
Does the vocabulary include a term newly coined in the NT period for this word — and if so, what does the coinage reveal?
What does the verbal form of the primary term reveal about the word's mode of operation?
Does the vocabulary include a spatial or architectural term — a place or structure associated with the word?
What does the semantic range of the primary term reveal about the breadth of the word's operation?
Does any term carry a somatic or physiological dimension — naming an inner organ or physical site as the word's location?
What does the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective) reveal about how the word is conceptualised — as object, action, or quality?
Does the vocabulary include a supplication term — a term for the act of seeking the word from another?
What does the supplication term reveal about the inner posture required to seek the word?
Does the vocabulary include a term for the mechanism by which the word is administered or conveyed?
What does the atonement or propitiation vocabulary (if present) reveal about the structural conditions under which the word operates?
Does any term reveal a classical-to-NT directional reversal — a term whose direction of operation changes between its classical use and its NT use?
What does the vocabulary reveal about whether the word operates primarily within or across relational boundaries?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's relationship to covenant — is it bounded by covenant or can it extend beyond?
Does any term in the vocabulary name the word as a divine possession — something that belongs to God and is located with him?
What does the root architecture of the vocabulary reveal about the word's conceptual relationship to adjacent characteristics?
Does the vocabulary include compound terms — and if so, what do the compounds reveal?
What does the vocabulary reveal about the word's temporal character — is it punctiliar, durative, or both?
Does the vocabulary reveal whether the word operates as a judicial category, a relational one, or both?
What does the most intensive or plural form of the vocabulary term reveal?
Does the vocabulary include terms that name the word's expression in speech?
Does the vocabulary include terms whose etymology connects to the body — and if so, to which part?
What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about the word's complete semantic range — from its most intimate to its most structural expression?
Does the vocabulary include a term that names the word's operation at the intersection of the human and divine — a term that holds both simultaneously?

Section 5 — Connections and Research Poi.question_text: What is the strongest co-occurrence connection for this word — which adjacent characteristic appears most frequently in the same verses — and what does the co-occurrence pattern reveal?
Does any verse indicate that this word and its most closely connected adjacent characteristic operate simultaneously in the same inner-being moment — and what would that simultaneity imply?
What are the three highest vocabulary-sharing registries for this word — and what does the pattern of sharing reveal about the word's structural position in the programme?
Does the vocabulary overlap with any adjacent characteristic extend to root-level architecture — a shared root rather than only shared individual terms?
How many of this word's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another cluster or characteristic — and is there any cluster that shares all confirmed dimensions?
Does any single verse make an explicit structural relationship between this word and an adjacent characteristic — naming the connection rather than merely exemplifying it?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and the characteristic with which it shares the most anchor verses — and what does that shared anchoring reveal?

Where this word and an adjacent characteristic share a dominant directionality (such as both being primarily divine-to-human), what does the shared directionality reveal about their structural relationship?
What is the relationship between this word and human will in the verse evidence — does the word displace, bypass, or reconstitute the will?
How does the study describe the relationship between this word and calling — is calling a consequence, an expression, or a distinct phenomenon?
What inner-being posture connects this word and listening — and is that posture described as a precondition or an expression of the word?
How does the study distinguish this word from faith — and what is their structural relationship?
What is the described sequence from the word to peace — and what comes between them?
What is the vocabulary overlap and co-occurrence count between this word and guilt?
What is the relationship between this word and mourning — does mourning precede, follow, or accompany the word?
Does the study present this word and hope as two separate phenomena or as aspects of a single inner-being posture?
Does any verse function as a primary anchor in both this word's study and another word's study — and what does that dual membership reveal about their relationship?
Does any verse anchor both this word and another word — and what shared inner-being posture does that dual anchoring reveal?
Does any verse present adjacent qualities as aspects of a unified inner-being posture — and if so, what is their common source?
What functional overlap does the verse evidence suggest between this word and wisdom?
Does the word operate only in relation to moral conditions (guilt) or also in relation to conditions of incapacity — and what is the evidence?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and goodness — and in what literary context does the pairing appear?
What is the co-occurrence count between this word and anointing, and between this word and blessing?
Is the connection between this word and forgiveness established through contextual inference or through the lexical form of the term itself?
Does the verse evidence in this study point toward a connection with repentance — and is that connection sufficiently examined or deferred?
Does a recurring posture appear across the word-seeking scenes in the study — and is its relationship to an adjacent characteristic examined or deferred?

Section 5 — Generic (gap addition R067).question_text: Where a registry's word functions in more than one dimension (both as a character attribute and as a comparative/evaluative operator), does the co-occurrence and correlation data reveal different connection patterns for different modes of the word?

generic_section_summary: Section 1 — Generic (gap addition R067 obslog v3)
